title: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler

items.title: Understanding Bacterial Meningitis: Definition and Key Organisms
items.content: Bacterial meningitis is defined as an acute purulent infection within the subarachnoid space, often leading to a CNS inflammatory reaction. This condition can result in severe complications such as decreased consciousness, seizures, raised ICP, and stroke. The most common organisms causing community-acquired bacterial meningitis include Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, group B streptococci, and Listeria monocytogenes.
keyPoints: Bacterial meningitis involves acute purulent infection in the subarachnoid space.
It can lead to CNS inflammatory reactions causing decreased consciousness, seizures, raised ICP, and stroke.
Annual incidence in the U.S. is ~1.4 cases/100,000 population.
Common organisms: Streptococcus pneumoniae (~50%), Neisseria meningitidis (~25%), group B streptococci (~15%), Listeria monocytogenes (~10%).
Haemophilus influenzae type b accounts for <10% of cases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiology and Patterns of Bacterial Meningitis Outbreaks
items.content: Bacterial meningitis is a significant suppurative CNS infection with an annual incidence of ~1.4 cases per 100,000 population in the U.S. Neisseria meningitidis is notable for causing recurring epidemics every 8–12 years.
keyPoints: Annual incidence: ~1.4 cases/100,000 population in the U.S.
Neisseria meningitidis causes recurring epidemics of meningitis every 8–12 years.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: S. pneumoniae: Leading Cause and Risk Factors in Adult Meningitis
items.content: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of meningitis in adults over 20 years old, accounting for nearly half of cases (1.1 per 100,000 persons annually). Predisposing conditions include pneumococcal pneumonia, sinusitis, otitis media, alcoholism, diabetes, splenectomy, hypogammaglobulinemia, complement deficiency, and head trauma with basilar skull fracture.
keyPoints: S. pneumoniae is the leading cause of adult meningitis (>20 years) with an incidence rate of 1.1 per 100,000 persons/year.
Risk factors include pneumococcal pneumonia, sinusitis, otitis media, alcoholism, diabetes, splenectomy, hypogammaglobulinemia, complement deficiency, and head trauma with basilar skull fracture.
Mortality rate remains ~20% despite antibiotic therapy.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.title: N. meningitidis: Impact of Vaccination on Incidence
items.content: The incidence of N. meningitidis meningitis has decreased due to routine immunization with quadrivalent and serogroup B vaccines in adolescents and young adults. Individuals treated with complement inhibitors or those with complement deficiencies should receive appropriate vaccination.
keyPoints: Routine immunization with quadrivalent (A, C, W-135, Y) and serogroup B vaccines has reduced N. meningitidis incidence.
Adolescents (11–18 years) receive the quadrivalent vaccine; young adults (16–23 years) receive the serogroup B vaccine.
Pentavalent vaccine covers A, B, C, W-135, Y and is recommended for individuals with complement inhibitors or deficiencies.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.title: L. monocytogenes: Increasing Importance in Specific Populations
items.content: Listeria monocytogenes is a significant cause of meningitis in neonates, pregnant women, individuals over 60, and immunocompromised persons. It is acquired through contaminated foods like coleslaw, milk, soft cheeses, and ready-to-eat meats.
keyPoints: L. monocytogenes causes meningitis in neonates (<1 month), pregnant women, >60 years, and immunocompromised individuals.
Transmission occurs via ingestion of contaminated food (coleslaw, milk, soft cheeses, ready-to-eat foods).
Increasingly important cause of meningitis in specified populations.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanism of Bacterial Meningitis Pathogenesis
items.content: Bacteria like S. pneumoniae and N. meningitidis colonize the nasopharynx, invade epithelial cells, and enter the bloodstream. They avoid phagocytosis due to their polysaccharide capsule and reach the CSF by adhering to cerebral capillary endothelial cells or infecting choroid plexus epithelial cells.
keyPoints: Bacteria attach to nasopharyngeal epithelial cells, invade intravascular space.
Polysaccharide capsule helps bacteria avoid phagocytosis and complement-mediated bactericidal activity.
CSF has few WBCs and low levels of complement proteins and immunoglobulins, impairing bacterial opsonization.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Inflammatory Response in Bacterial Meningitis
items.content: The inflammatory response to bacteria is a critical event in meningitis pathogenesis. Cytokines like TNF-α and IL-1β increase blood-brain barrier permeability, leading to vasogenic edema and protein leakage into the SAS.
keyPoints: Cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-1β) occurs within 1–2 hours of LPS inoculation.
Increased CSF protein concentration and leukocytosis follow cytokine response.
Inflammatory cytokines increase blood-brain barrier permeability, causing vasogenic edema.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Consequences of Elevated CSF Cytokines in Meningitis
items.content: Elevated CSF cytokines lead to increased blood-brain barrier permeability, obstructive hydrocephalus, and cerebral edema. Inflammatory responses can cause ischemia, infarction, and raised ICP.
keyPoints: TNF-α and IL-1β synergistically increase blood-brain barrier permeability.
Proteinaceous exudate in SAS obstructs CSF flow, causing hydrocephalus.
Cytokines lead to cerebral edema, ischemia, infarction, and raised ICP.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Meningitis: Key Clinical Features and Red Flags
items.content: Meningitis can present acutely or subacutely, with fever, headache, and nuchal rigidity being key features. A decreased level of consciousness is common, occurring in >75% of patients. Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs are classic but may be absent in certain populations.
keyPoints: Fever, headache, and nuchal rigidity occur in >80% of adult cases of acute bacterial meningitis.
Decreased level of consciousness occurs in >75% of patients.
Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs may be absent or reduced in young, elderly, immunocompromised individuals, or those with depressed mental status.
Seizures occur in 15–40% of patients during bacterial meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Identifying Raised Intracranial Pressure (ICP) and Its Consequences in Meningitis
items.content: Raised ICP is a major complication of bacterial meningitis, leading to obtundation and coma. Most patients have CSF opening pressures >180 mmH₂O, with some exceeding 400 mmH₂O. Signs include deteriorating consciousness, papilledema, and the Cushing reflex.
keyPoints: More than 90% of patients will have a CSF opening pressure >180 mmH₂O.
20% have opening pressures >400 mmH₂O.
Signs of increased ICP include deteriorating consciousness, papilledema, and the Cushing reflex.
Cerebral herniation incidence ranges from 1% to 8% in bacterial meningitis cases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Initiation and Timing of Dexamethasone in Bacterial Meningitis
items.content: When bacterial meningitis is suspected, empirical antimicrobial therapy should be started immediately after obtaining blood cultures. Dexamethasone administration should ideally begin 20 minutes before or concurrently with the first dose of antibiotics to reduce complications.
keyPoints: Empirical antimicrobial and dexamethasone therapy initiated without delay
Dexamethasone starts ideally 20 min before, or not later than concurrent with, the first antibiotic dose
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: CSF Findings in Bacterial Meningitis
items.content: The diagnosis of bacterial meningitis is confirmed by CSF analysis. Key CSF abnormalities include PMN leukocytosis (>100 cells/μL), decreased glucose concentration (<2.2 mmol/L or <40 mg/dL), increased protein concentration (>0.45 g/L), and elevated opening pressure (>180 mmH₂O).
keyPoints: PMN leukocytosis >100 cells/μL in 90%
Decreased glucose concentration <2.2 mmol/L (<40 mg/dL) or CSF/serum ratio <0.4
Increased protein concentration >0.45 g/L (>45 mg/dL)
Elevated opening pressure >180 mmH₂O
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Use of CSF Multiplex PCR in Bacterial Meningitis
items.content: CSF multiplex PCR assays, such as the FilmArray panel, detect nucleic acids of common bacterial pathogens like S. pneumoniae but do not include all potential pathogens. While rapid, these assays cannot replace culture for antimicrobial susceptibility testing.
keyPoints: FilmArray Meningitis/Encephalitis panel detects S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, E. coli, L. monocytogenes, H. influenzae, and S. agalactiae
PCR assays do not include S. aureus, coagulase-negative staphylococci, many gram-negatives
Culture required for antimicrobial susceptibility testing
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Distinguishing HSV Encephalitis from Bacterial Meningitis
items.content: HSV encephalitis and bacterial meningitis can present similarly, but key diagnostic differences exist. CSF studies show lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose in viral infections versus PMN pleocytosis and hypoglycorrhachia in bacterial cases. MRI findings of high-signal-intensity lesions in specific brain regions are indicative of HSV encephalitis.
keyPoints: HSV encephalitis presents with headache, fever, altered consciousness, focal neurologic deficits, seizures
CSF profile: lymphocytic pleocytosis and normal glucose for viral infections; PMN pleocytosis and hypoglycorrhachia for bacterial meningitis
CSF HSV PCR sensitivity is 96% and specificity is 99% when examined 72 hours after symptom onset
MRI shows high-signal-intensity lesions in the orbitofrontal, anterior, and medial temporal lobes within 48 hours of HSV encephalitis onset
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Identifying Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (RMSF) vs. Bacterial Meningitis
items.content: RMSF can mimic bacterial meningitis but is distinguished by its characteristic rash and tick exposure history. The rash progresses from erythematous to petechial, then purpuric, and may lead to skin necrosis if untreated.
keyPoints: RMSF presents with high fever, prostration, myalgia, headache, nausea, vomiting
Characteristic rash appears within 96 hours: starts on wrists/ankles, spreads distally/proximally, involves palms and soles
Diagnosis via immunofluorescent staining of skin biopsy specimens
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Considering Focal Suppurative CNS Infections in Meningitis with Neurologic Deficits
items.content: In cases of suspected meningitis with focal neurologic signs, consider focal suppurative CNS infections like subdural or epidural empyema and brain abscess. Prompt MRI is recommended to detect intracranial infection and associated sinus/mastoid bone infections.
keyPoints: Focal neurologic findings in suspected meningitis warrant consideration of focal suppurative CNS infections
MRI should be performed promptly to identify intracranial infections and related areas of infection
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Initiating Empirical Therapy for Bacterial Meningitis
items.content: Empirical antimicrobial therapy should be started within 60 minutes of a patient's arrival in the emergency room when bacterial meningitis is suspected. This includes a combination of dexamethasone, a third- or fourth-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, or cefepime), and vancomycin. Acyclovir should be added to cover HSV encephalitis, and doxycycline during tick season for tick-borne infections.
keyPoints: Begin antibiotic therapy within 60 minutes of arrival in the emergency room.
Common etiologic organisms: S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis.
Empirical therapy includes dexamethasone, third- or fourth-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, cefepime), vancomycin, acyclovir, and doxycycline during tick season.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · EMPIRICAL ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.title: Empirical Therapy for Hospital-Acquired Meningitis
items.content: For hospital-acquired meningitis, especially post-neurosurgical procedures, empirical therapy should include vancomycin and either ceftazidime or meropenem. Ceftazidime or meropenem is preferred over ceftriaxone and cefotaxime due to inadequate activity against P. aeruginosa.
keyPoints: Hospital-acquired meningitis often involves staphylococci and gram-negative organisms like P. aeruginosa.
Empirical therapy should include vancomycin plus ceftazidime or meropenem.
Ceftazidime or meropenem is preferred over ceftriaxone and cefotaxime for neurosurgical patients due to better coverage against P. aeruginosa.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · EMPIRICAL ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.title: Penicillin Susceptibility in Meningococcal Meningitis
items.content: For meningococcal meningitis, penicillin G is the preferred antibiotic for susceptible strains. However, due to increasing resistance, CSF isolates should be tested for susceptibility. If resistant, cefotaxime or ceftriaxone are recommended.
keyPoints: Penicillin G is preferred for meningococcal meningitis caused by susceptible N. meningitidis strains.
CSF isolates should be tested for penicillin and ampicillin susceptibility.
If resistance is found, substitute with cefotaxime or ceftriaxone.
A 7-day course of IV antibiotic therapy is adequate for uncomplicated cases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.title: Antibiotic Selection in Pneumococcal Meningitis
items.content: Initial treatment for pneumococcal meningitis involves ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, or vancomycin. Susceptibility testing guides therapy adjustments. Vancomycin is preferred if MIC >1 μg/mL. A repeat LP after 24–36 h assesses CSF sterilization.
keyPoints: Initial therapy: cephalosporin (ceftriaxone, cefotaxime) + vancomycin.
S. pneumoniae susceptibility: Penicillin MIC <0.06 μg/mL is sensitive; >0.12 μg/mL is resistant.
Cephalosporins MIC ≤0.5 μg/mL are sensitive; ≥2 μg/mL are resistant.
Vancomycin preferred for cefotaxime/ceftriaxone MIC >1 μg/mL.
Repeat LP after 24–36 h to check CSF sterilization.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.title: Treatment Duration and Monitoring in Pneumococcal Meningitis
items.content: A 2-week IV course is recommended for pneumococcal meningitis. If CSF sterilization fails after 24–36 h, consider resistance. Intraventricular vancomycin may be added for resistant strains.
keyPoints: 2-week IV antibiotic therapy is recommended.
Repeat LP after 24–36 h to confirm CSF sterilization.
Failure to sterilize suggests antibiotic resistance.
Intraventricular vancomycin can be added if resistant strains do not respond to IV vancomycin.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.title: Timing of Dexamethasone Administration in Bacterial Meningitis
items.content: Dexamethasone is most effective when administered before the onset of bacterial meningitis symptoms, specifically 20 minutes prior to antibiotic therapy. This timing inhibits TNF-α production by macrophages and microglia, reducing meningeal inflammation and neurologic sequelae.
keyPoints: Dexamethasone inhibits IL-1β and TNF-α synthesis at the mRNA level.
Administer dexamethasone 20 min before antibiotics to prevent TNF-α production by activated cells.
Clinical trials show decreased unfavorable outcomes (15% vs. 25%, p = .03) with dexamethasone in pneumococcal meningitis.
Dexamethasone is unlikely to benefit if started >6 hours after antibiotic initiation.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY

items.title: Considerations for Dexamethasone Use in Different Settings
items.content: The efficacy of dexamethasone varies by setting, showing benefits in high-income countries but not in low-income regions. Factors such as late hospital presentation and malnutrition contribute to its lack of efficacy in these areas.
keyPoints: Dexamethasone reduces hearing loss and death rates in meningococcal meningitis without adverse effects.
In sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia, dexamethasone showed no benefit due to late presentation and advanced disease.
Patients with negative CSF Gram's stain and culture in low-income countries should not receive dexamethasone.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Management and Prognosis of Increased Intracranial Pressure (ICP)
items.content: Emergency management of increased ICP includes head elevation, intubation, hyperventilation to a Paco2 of 25–30 mmHg, mannitol administration, and intensive care unit monitoring with ICP devices. Prognosis is influenced by factors such as decreased consciousness on admission, early seizures, signs of increased ICP, age extremes, comorbid conditions, and delayed treatment initiation.
keyPoints: Elevation of head to 30–45°
Hyperventilation target Paco2: 25–30 mmHg
Mannitol administration
ICP monitoring in ICU
Prognostic factors include decreased consciousness, early seizures, increased ICP signs, age extremes, comorbid conditions, delayed treatment
Mortality rates: H. influenzae, N. meningitidis, group B streptococci (3–7%), L. monocytogenes (15%), S. pneumoniae (20%)
CSF glucose <2.2 mmol/L (<40 mg/dL) and CSF protein >3 g/L (>300 mg/dL) predict poor outcomes
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Viral Meningitis
items.content: Immunocompetent adults with viral meningitis typically present with headache, fever, and meningeal irritation signs. Headache is common, often frontal or retroorbital, with photophobia and eye movement pain. Nuchal rigidity may be mild. Constitutional symptoms include malaise, myalgia, anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and mild lethargy. Severe consciousness alterations, seizures, focal neurologic signs, or imaging abnormalities suggest encephalitis or other CNS processes.
keyPoints: Headache is almost invariably present
Fever and meningeal irritation signs
Nuchal rigidity may be mild
Constitutional symptoms: malaise, myalgia, anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea
Mild lethargy or drowsiness
Severe consciousness alterations suggest encephalitis or other CNS processes
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Diagnostic Yield of CSF PCR in Viral Meningitis
items.content: CSF PCR testing is a critical diagnostic tool for identifying viral causes in cases of meningitis, with a high success rate.
keyPoints: Specific viral cause identified in 60–90% of viral meningitis cases using CSF PCR, culture, and serology.
Enteroviruses, VZV, HSV (HSV-2 > HSV-1), HIV, and arboviruses are important agents.
CSF cultures positive in 30–70% of patients; two-thirds of culture-negative cases identified by CSF PCR.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Seasonal Incidence of Viral Meningitis
items.content: Viral meningitis incidence peaks in nonwinter months, correlating with enterovirus and arbovirus activity.
keyPoints: Incidence estimated at 75,000 cases per year in the U.S.
Substantial increase in cases during summer and fall.
Peak monthly incidence of about 1 case per 100,000 population.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: CSF Profile in Viral Meningitis
items.content: The CSF profile for viral meningitis typically includes pleocytosis with a predominance of lymphocytes, normal to slightly elevated protein levels, and normal glucose concentration. Opening pressure is usually within the range of 100–350 mmH₂O.
keyPoints: Pleocytosis: total CSF cell count typically 25–500/μL
Protein concentration: 0.2–0.8 g/L (20–80 mg/dL)
Glucose concentration: normal
Opening pressure: 100–350 mmH₂O
Predominant cells: lymphocytes, though PMNs may predominate initially in some infections
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Indications for Alternative Diagnoses Based on CSF Findings
items.content: A CSF profile showing PMN pleocytosis, especially with low glucose levels, should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses such as bacterial meningitis or other infections like CMV in immunocompromised patients.
keyPoints: PMN pleocytosis: seen in 45% of WNV meningitis cases
Low CSF glucose concentration: may indicate fungal or tuberculosis meningitis, Listeria meningoencephalitis, or noninfectious disorders
Alternative diagnoses consideration: bacterial meningitis or parameningeal infections
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Role of PCR in Diagnosing CNS Viral Infections
items.content: PCR amplification of viral nucleic acids from CSF is the most important method for diagnosing CNS viral infections, offering higher sensitivity than viral cultures. It is particularly useful for HSV and enteroviral infections.
keyPoints: CSF PCR: more sensitive than viral cultures
Useful in recurrent 'aseptic' meningitis with amplifiable HSV DNA
FilmArray Meningitis/Encephalitis panel includes HSV-1, HSV-2, enteroviruses, VZV, HHV-6, and human Parechovirus
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: When to Consider Neuroimaging in Suspected Viral Meningitis
items.content: Neuroimaging, preferably MRI over CT, is not mandatory for uncomplicated viral meningitis but is recommended if the patient presents with altered consciousness, seizures, focal neurological signs or symptoms, atypical CSF profiles, or has underlying immunocompromising conditions.
keyPoints: MRI preferred to CT in certain cases
Indications: altered consciousness, seizures, focal neurologic signs/symptoms, atypical CSF profiles, immunocompromising conditions
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differentiating Bacterial from Aseptic Meningitis in Children
items.content: In children >28 days, CSF protein levels >0.5 g/L and serum procalcitonin levels >0.5 ng/mL suggest bacterial meningitis over aseptic. The bacterial meningitis score helps determine the probability of bacterial infection based on specific CSF and clinical criteria.
keyPoints: CSF protein >0.5 g/L (sensitivity 89%, specificity 78%)
Serum procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL (sensitivity 89%, specificity 89%)

Bacterial meningitis score: probability <0.3% with negative CSF Gram's stain, CSF neutrophil count <1000 cells/μL, CSF protein <80 mg/dL, peripheral absolute neutrophil count <10,000 cells/μL, no seizures
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Enteroviruses as the Leading Cause of Viral Meningitis
items.content: Enteroviruses are responsible for over 85% of viral meningitis cases with a specific etiology. They primarily affect children under 15 years, especially during summer and fall, but can also occur sporadically in adults.
keyPoints: Enteroviruses account for >85% of identified viral meningitis cases.
Most common in children <15 years, particularly in summer and fall months.
CSF profile: lymphocytic pleocytosis (100–1000 cells/μL), normal glucose, normal or mildly elevated protein.
Up to 15% may have a normal CSF leukocyte count, especially young infants.
RT-PCR is the diagnostic procedure of choice with sensitivity >95%, best within 48 hours of symptom onset.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.title: Arbovirus Infections and Seasonal Considerations
items.content: Arboviruses, including WNV, are significant causes of meningitis and encephalitis in summer and early fall. Clusters in specific regions should raise suspicion for arboviral infections.
keyPoints: Predominantly occur in summer and early fall.
WNV, St. Louis encephalitis virus, and California encephalitis group are key causes in the U.S.
CSF profile: lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, normal or mildly elevated protein.
~45% of WNV meningitis patients have CSF neutrophilia.
Definitive diagnosis via viral-specific IgM in CSF or seroconversion.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Approaches for Arboviral Meningitis
items.content: Diagnosis of arboviral meningitis relies on CSF IgM detection or seroconversion, with RT-PCR available but less sensitive than serology for WNV.
keyPoints: CSF IgM prevalence increases to >80% in neuroinvasive disease within the first week.
Repeat studies may be needed if initial tests are negative and suspicion remains high.
WNV CSF RT-PCR sensitivity is ~70%, less than serology, but useful in immunocompromised patients.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.title: HSV Meningitis: A Major Cause of Viral Meningitis in Adults
items.content: Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) is a significant cause of viral meningitis, particularly HSV-2 in adults. It accounts for about 5% of all cases and occurs more frequently outside the enterovirus season. Diagnosis relies on CSF PCR due to its higher sensitivity compared to cultures.
keyPoints: HSV meningitis accounts for ~5% of total viral meningitis cases.
HSV-2 is predominant in adults, while HSV-1 causes 90% of encephalitis cases.
25–35% of women and 10–15% of men experience meningitis during primary genital herpes infection.
20% of these patients have recurrent meningitis attacks.
CSF PCR is the preferred diagnostic method over cultures, which may be negative in recurrent cases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.title: VZV Meningitis: Consider Even Without Rash
items.content: Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV) can cause meningitis and encephalitis, sometimes without rash. Its prevalence in meningitis cases varies widely. Diagnosis is primarily through CSF PCR, supplemented by serologic studies.
keyPoints: VZV causes 3–20% of meningitis cases depending on the series.
CSF PCR is used for diagnosis, though less sensitive than for other herpesviruses.
Serologic studies and detection of VZV-specific intrathecal antibodies or IgM are useful.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.title: HIV Meningitis: Consider in High-Risk Patients
items.content: HIV meningitis should be suspected in patients with viral meningitis who have risk factors for HIV. It can occur during primary infection or later stages, often presenting with cranial nerve palsies.
keyPoints: Meningitis occurs in 5–10% of cases following primary HIV infection.
Cranial nerve palsies are more common than in other viral infections.
Diagnosis is confirmed by detecting the HIV genome in blood or CSF.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis
items.title: Consider LCMV in Aseptic Meningitis with Specific Season and Exposure History
items.content: LCMV infection should be considered when aseptic meningitis occurs in late fall or winter, especially if there is a history of exposure to house mice or other rodents. Diagnosis can be supported by clinical findings such as rash, pulmonary infiltrates, alopecia, parotitis, orchitis, myopericarditis, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, abnormal liver function tests, marked CSF pleocytosis (>1000 cells/μL), and hypoglycorrhachia (<30%).
keyPoints: Consider LCMV in aseptic meningitis with late fall or winter occurrence.
History of exposure to house mice or other rodents is significant.
Clinical findings: rash, pulmonary infiltrates, alopecia, parotitis, orchitis, myopericarditis.
Laboratory clues: leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, abnormal liver function tests.
CSF pleocytosis >1000 cells/μL and hypoglycorrhachia <30%.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis
items.title: Management of Viral Meningitis: Symptomatic Treatment and Hospitalization Criteria
items.content: Treatment for viral meningitis is primarily symptomatic, involving analgesics, antipyretics, and antiemetics. Immunocompetent patients without focal signs or significant consciousness alteration can be managed at home with proper monitoring. Hospitalization is recommended for immunocompromised patients, those with altered consciousness, seizures, or atypical CSF profiles. Acyclovir may benefit HSV-1/2, severe EBV, or VZV infections. Immunodeficient patients should receive intramuscular or IVIg.
keyPoints: Symptomatic treatment: analgesics, antipyretics, antiemetics.
Monitor fluid and electrolyte status.
Hospitalization for immunocompromised, altered consciousness, seizures, focal signs, atypical CSF.
Acyclovir (15–30 mg/kg/day IV) followed by oral acyclovir, famciclovir, or valacyclovir for 7–14 days.
Intramuscular or IVIg for patients with deficient humoral immunity.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis

items.title: Prognosis of Viral Meningitis in Adults vs. Infants
items.content: The prognosis for full recovery from viral meningitis is excellent in adults, though some may experience persisting symptoms like headache and mild mental impairment. In contrast, the outcome in infants and neonates is less certain, with potential lasting sequelae such as intellectual impairment.
keyPoints: Excellent prognosis for full recovery in adults
Persisting symptoms in rare adult cases: headache, mild mental impairment, incoordination, generalized asthenia
Less certain outcome in infants and neonates (<1 year)
Possible lasting sequelae in infants: intellectual impairment, learning disabilities, hearing loss
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Etiology of Subacute Meningitis and Pathogen Characteristics
items.content: Subacute meningitis often presents with unrelenting headache, stiff neck, low-grade fever, and lethargy. Common pathogens include M. tuberculosis, C. neoformans, H. capsulatum, C. immitis, and T. pallidum, each with distinct acquisition methods and pathophysiology.
keyPoints: Subacute meningitis symptoms: unrelenting headache, stiff neck, low-grade fever, lethargy
Common pathogens: M. tuberculosis, C. neoformans, H. capsulatum, C. immitis, T. pallidum
M. tuberculosis acquired by inhalation of aerosolized droplet nuclei
Tuberculous meningitis develops from caseous tubercles in the brain parenchyma
Fungal infections acquired by inhalation of airborne spores
C. neoformans found worldwide in soil and bird excreta
H. capsulatum endemic to Ohio and Mississippi River valleys, Central/South America
C. immitis endemic to desert areas of southwest US, northern Mexico, Argentina
Syphilis: T. pallidum invades CNS early, often affecting cranial nerves VII and VIII
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Diagnosing Tuberculous Meningitis: Key CSF Findings and Diagnostic Strategies
items.content: In tuberculous meningitis, characteristic cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) abnormalities include elevated opening pressure, lymphocytic pleocytosis (10–500 cells/μL), elevated protein concentration (1–5 g/L), and decreased glucose concentration (1.1–2.2 mmol/L or 20–40 mg/dL). The presence of these CSF findings along with clinical symptoms like headache, stiff neck, fatigue, night sweats, and fever strongly suggests tuberculous meningitis. Acid-fast bacilli (AFB) smears from the last tube collected at lumbar puncture are most useful for diagnosis, although positive results occur in only 10–40% of cases. CSF cultures remain the gold standard but take 4–8 weeks to yield results and are positive in about 50% of adults.
keyPoints: CSF findings: elevated opening pressure, lymphocytic pleocytosis (10–500 cells/μL), protein concentration (1–5 g/L), glucose concentration (1.1–2.2 mmol/L or 20–40 mg/dL)
AFB smear positive in 10-40% of cases
CSF cultures take 4-8 weeks, positive in ~50% of adults
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis: Diagnostic Tests and CSF Findings
items.content: Cryptococcal meningitis is characterized by a mononuclear or lymphocytic pleocytosis, increased protein concentration, and decreased glucose concentration in the CSF. The cryptococcal polysaccharide antigen test is highly sensitive and specific for diagnosing this condition; a reactive result confirms the diagnosis. Although large volumes of CSF may be needed to demonstrate the organism on India ink smear or culture, the antigen test provides a reliable diagnostic tool.
keyPoints: CSF findings: mononuclear/lymphocytic pleocytosis, increased protein, decreased glucose
Cryptococcal polysaccharide antigen test is highly sensitive and specific
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Empirical Therapy for Tuberculous Meningitis
items.content: Initiate empirical therapy based on high suspicion, using a combination of isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol, and pyridoxine. Adjust treatment duration and components based on clinical response and CSF culture results.
keyPoints: Initial therapy: Isoniazid (300 mg/d), Rifampin (10 mg/kg per day), Pyrazinamide (30 mg/kg per day), Ethambutol (15–25 mg/kg per day), Pyridoxine (50 mg/d)
Discontinue ethambutol if antimicrobial sensitivity is known
Continue Isoniazid and Rifampin for 6–12 months after discontinuing pyrazinamide at 8 weeks
Prolong therapy to 9–12 months for inadequate symptom resolution or positive CSF cultures
Dexamethasone recommended for HIV-negative patients: 12–16 mg/d for 3 weeks, then tapered over 3 weeks
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.title: Treatment of Cryptococcal Meningitis in Non-HIV Patients
items.content: Use induction therapy with amphotericin B and flucytosine, followed by consolidation therapy with fluconazole. Adjust treatment based on CSF culture results.
keyPoints: Induction: AmB (0.7 mg/kg IV per day) + Flucytosine (100 mg/kg per day in four divided doses) for at least 4 weeks
Extend to 6 weeks if neurologic complications arise
Consolidation: Fluconazole 400 mg/d for 8 weeks
Follow CSF yeast cultures, not cryptococcal antigen titer
Sterile CSF after 10 weeks allows fluconazole reduction to 200 mg/d for 6 months to a year
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.title: Management of Syphilitic Meningitis
items.content: Administer aqueous or procaine penicillin G with probenecid, followed by benzathine penicillin G. Monitor CSF for treatment success.
keyPoints: Aqueous Penicillin G: 3–4 million units IV every 4 hours for 10–14 days
Alternative: Procaine Penicillin G (2.4 million units IM daily) + Probenecid (500 mg orally four times daily)
Follow with Benzathine Penicillin G (2.4 million units IM once a week for 3 weeks)
Reexamine CSF at 6-month intervals for 2 years
Success: Normalized cell count within 12 months, VDRL titer decrease by two dilutions or revert to nonreactive within 2 years
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.question: Which organism is most frequently responsible for community-acquired bacterial meningitis?
items.explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae is responsible for approximately 50% of community-acquired bacterial meningitis cases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the approximate annual incidence of bacterial meningitis in the United States?
items.explanation: The annual incidence of bacterial meningitis in the United States is approximately ~1.4 cases per 100,000 population.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which organism accounts for less than 10% of bacterial meningitis cases?
items.explanation: Haemophilus influenzae type b accounts for less than 10% of bacterial meningitis cases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which organism is the most common cause of meningitis in adults over 20 years of age?
items.explanation: S. pneumoniae is the most common cause of meningitis in adults over 20 years, accounting for nearly half the reported cases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.question: What are some predisposing conditions that increase the risk of pneumococcal meningitis?
items.explanation: Predisposing conditions include pneumococcal pneumonia, diabetes, and splenectomy among others.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which vaccine is recommended for individuals being treated with complement inhibitors to prevent meningococcal disease?
options: Both quadrivalent and serogroup B meningococcal vaccines or pentavalent meningococcal vaccine
items.explanation: Individuals treated with complement inhibitors should receive either the quadrivalent vaccine plus a serogroup B meningococcal vaccine, or the pentavalent meningococcal vaccine.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which condition is associated with an increased risk of meningitis due to gram-negative bacilli?
items.explanation: Gram-negative bacilli cause meningitis in individuals with chronic diseases, neurosurgical procedures, and head trauma associated with CSF rhinorrhea.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which mechanism allows S. pneumoniae to avoid phagocytosis by neutrophils?
options: Attachment to cerebral capillary endothelial cells
items.explanation: The presence of a polysaccharide capsule helps bacteria avoid phagocytosis by neutrophils.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What is the initial step in the induction of the inflammatory response during bacterial meningitis?
options: Adherence to cerebral capillary endothelial cells
Lysis of bacteria and release of cell-wall components
Migration through or between cerebral capillary endothelial cells
items.explanation: The lysis of bacteria with the subsequent release of cell-wall components into the subarachnoid space initiates the inflammatory response.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which cytokines are involved in increasing the permeability of the blood-brain barrier during bacterial meningitis?
options: Tumor necrosis factor alpha and interleukin 1 beta
Interferon alpha and Tumor necrosis factor beta
items.explanation: TNF-α and IL-1β act synergistically to increase the permeability of the blood-brain barrier.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What is a consequence of elevated levels of CSF cytokines and chemokines in bacterial meningitis?
options: Decrease in leukocyte adherence to vascular endothelial cells
Reduction in the permeability of blood vessels
Induction of vasogenic edema and leakage of serum proteins into the SAS
items.explanation: Elevated levels of CSF cytokines and chemokines lead to increased permeability, resulting in vasogenic edema and protein leakage.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is considered a pathognomonic sign of meningeal irritation?
items.explanation: Nuchal rigidity, or 'stiff neck,' is the pathognomonic sign of meningeal irritation.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What percentage of patients with acute bacterial meningitis typically present with a decreased level of consciousness?
items.explanation: A decreased level of consciousness occurs in >75% of patients with acute bacterial meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: Which complication is the major cause of obtundation and coma in bacterial meningitis?
items.explanation: Raised intracranial pressure (ICP) is the major cause of obtundation and coma in bacterial meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: In the management of suspected bacterial meningitis, when should dexamethasone therapy ideally be initiated relative to antibiotic administration?
options: 20 minutes after the first dose of antibiotics
Simultaneously with or before the first dose of antibiotics
After obtaining blood cultures but before LP
Only if neuroimaging studies are negative
items.explanation: Dexamethasone should ideally be started 20 minutes before, or not later than concurrent with, the first dose of antibiotics.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which CSF finding is most suggestive of bacterial meningitis?
items.explanation: An increased opening pressure (>180 mmH₂O) is a classic CSF abnormality in bacterial meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What should be done if lumbar puncture (LP) is delayed to obtain neuroimaging studies in suspected bacterial meningitis?
options: Withhold empirical antibiotic therapy until LP is performed
Administer antibiotics after obtaining blood cultures
Perform LP without prior neuroimaging regardless of the delay
items.explanation: If LP is delayed, empirical antibiotic therapy should be initiated after blood cultures are obtained.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following organisms is NOT detected by the FilmArray Meningitis/Encephalitis PCR panel?
items.explanation: The CSF multiplex PCR pathogen assays do not include S. aureus.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is a typical CSF finding in viral CNS infections such as HSV encephalitis?
options: Lymphocytic pleocytosis with a normal glucose concentration
Elevated protein levels with decreased glucose
Bacterial presence confirmed by Gram stain
items.explanation: Viral CNS infections typically present with lymphocytic pleocytosis and a normal glucose concentration.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which MRI finding is characteristic of HSV encephalitis?
options: High-signal-intensity lesions in the orbitofrontal and medial temporal lobes
items.explanation: HSV encephalitis typically shows high-signal-intensity lesions in the orbitofrontal, anterior, and medial temporal lobes on MRI.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of Rocky Mountain spotted fever (RMSF) rash progression?
options: It begins in the trunk and spreads to extremities.
It starts as petechial and progresses directly to gangrene.
The color changes from bright red to yellowish-green to black.
It remains static without changing over time.
items.explanation: RMSF rash changes color from bright red to very dark red, then yellowish-green to black.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method is used for confirming RMSF?
options: Immunofluorescent staining of skin biopsy specimens
items.explanation: RMSF diagnosis is made by immunofluorescent staining of skin biopsy specimens.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which combination of drugs is recommended for empirical therapy of community-acquired suspected bacterial meningitis in children and adults due to the emergence of penicillin- and cephalosporin-resistant S. pneumoniae?
options: Dexamethasone, a third- or fourth-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone), vancomycin, acyclovir, and doxycycline
items.explanation: The recommended combination includes dexamethasone, a third- or fourth-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone), vancomycin, acyclovir, and doxycycline.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · EMPIRICAL ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: Which antibiotic should be added to the empirical regimen for coverage of L. monocytogenes in individuals <3 months of age, those >55, or those with suspected impaired cell-mediated immunity?
items.explanation: Ampicillin should be added for coverage of L. monocytogenes in these populations.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · EMPIRICAL ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: In hospital-acquired meningitis, particularly after neurosurgical procedures, which combination is recommended as empirical therapy?
items.explanation: Empirical therapy should include a combination of vancomycin and ceftazidime or meropenem.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · EMPIRICAL ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: Which antibiotic regimen is recommended as chemoprophylaxis for close contacts of a patient with meningococcal meningitis?
options: Rifampin 600 mg every 12 h for 2 days in adults
Penicillin G 1 million units intravenously daily
items.explanation: Rifampin is recommended for chemoprophylaxis in close contacts of a patient with meningococcal meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: For pneumococcal meningitis, which antibiotic should be used if the cefotaxime or ceftriaxone MIC is >1 μg/mL?
items.explanation: Vancomycin is the antibiotic of choice for pneumococcal meningitis when cefotaxime or ceftriaxone MICs are >1 μg/mL.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: What is the recommended duration of intravenous antibiotic therapy for uncomplicated meningococcal meningitis?
items.explanation: A 7-day course of intravenous antibiotic therapy is adequate for uncomplicated meningococcal meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: Which combination of antibiotics is recommended for treating Listeria monocytogenes meningitis in critically ill patients?
items.explanation: For critically ill patients with Listeria meningitis, ampicillin is combined with gentamicin.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: What is the primary mechanism by which dexamethasone exerts its beneficial effect in bacterial meningitis?
options: Inhibiting the synthesis of IL-1 β and TNF-α at the level of mRNA, decreasing CSF outflow resistance, and stabilizing the blood-brain barrier
Increasing the production of inflammatory cytokines to enhance immune response
Directly killing bacteria by disrupting their cell walls
Enhancing antibiotic penetration into the cerebrospinal fluid
items.explanation: Dexamethasone inhibits the synthesis of IL-1 β and TNF-α at the level of mRNA, decreases CSF outflow resistance, and stabilizes the blood-brain barrier.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY

items.question: When should dexamethasone ideally be administered in relation to antibiotic therapy for bacterial meningitis?
options: After antibiotics have been initiated, at least 6 hours later
Concurrently with the first dose of antibiotics
20 minutes before or not later than concurrent with the first dose of antibiotics
Only after CSF sterilization has occurred
items.explanation: Dexamethasone should ideally be started 20 minutes before, or not later than concurrent with, the first dose of antibiotics.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY

items.question: Why is dexamethasone unlikely to be beneficial if administered more than 6 hours after starting antimicrobial therapy?
options: It increases hippocampal cell injury and reduces learning capacity
It does not alter TNF-α production once it has been induced
It enhances the penetration of vancomycin into CSF
It delays the sterilization of CSF in experimental models
items.explanation: Dexamethasone does not alter TNF-α production once it has been induced, making it ineffective if started more than 6 hours after antimicrobial therapy.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.question: Which intervention is NOT part of the emergency treatment for increased intracranial pressure (ICP)?
options: Elevation of the patient's head to 30–45°
Intubation and hyperventilation with Paco at 25–30 mmHg
items.explanation: Antibiotics are not part of the emergency treatment for increased ICP.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.question: Which factor is associated with an increased risk of death from bacterial meningitis?
options: Absence of seizures within 24 hours of admission
Decreased level of consciousness on admission
items.explanation: A decreased level of consciousness on admission is associated with an increased risk of death from bacterial meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation in immunocompetent adult patients with viral meningitis?
options: Profound alterations in consciousness such as stupor or coma
Headache often characterized as frontal or retroorbital
Neuroimaging abnormalities indicating brain parenchymal involvement
items.explanation: Headache, often characterized as frontal or retroorbital, is a common clinical manifestation in viral meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which diagnostic technique is most likely to identify the specific viral cause in cases of viral meningitis where CSF cultures are negative?
items.explanation: CSF PCR testing is used to identify the specific viral etiology in about two-thirds of culture-negative cases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which virus is more frequently associated with causing viral meningitis, HSV-1 or HSV-2?
items.explanation: HSV-2 is more frequently associated with viral meningitis than HSV-1.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What percentage of cases of viral meningitis have a specific viral cause identified using diagnostic techniques?
items.explanation: A specific viral cause can be found in 60–90% of cases using diagnostic techniques.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a typical CSF profile finding in viral meningitis?
options: PMN pleocytosis with low glucose concentration
Lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose concentration
Bacterial organisms seen on Gram's stain
items.explanation: Viral meningitis typically presents with lymphocytic pleocytosis and a normal glucose concentration.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: In the context of viral meningitis, what does the presence of PMN pleocytosis in CSF suggest?
options: It is a definitive indicator of viral meningitis
It should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses like bacterial meningitis
It confirms infection with lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV)
It indicates normal findings in viral infections
items.explanation: PMN pleocytosis in suspected viral meningitis should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses, including bacterial meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which test has become the most important method for diagnosing CNS viral infections?
options: Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) amplification of viral nucleic acid
items.explanation: PCR amplification of viral-specific DNA or RNA from CSF has become the most important method for diagnosing CNS viral infections.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which viruses are included in the FilmArray Meningitis/Encephalitis panel?
options: HSV-1, HSV-2, enteroviruses, VZV, HHV-6, human Parechovirus
items.explanation: The FilmArray Meningitis/Encephalitis panel includes HSV-1, HSV-2, enteroviruses, VZV, HHV-6, and human Parechovirus.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which neuroimaging study is preferable for evaluating patients with suspected viral meningitis who have altered consciousness or seizures?
items.explanation: MRI is preferable to CT in these cases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In the differential diagnosis of viral meningitis, which condition can present with a lymphocytic pleocytosis and potentially negative cultures in its early stages?
items.explanation: Early stages of fungal, mycobacterial, or neurosyphilis meningitis can present with these features.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which clinical parameter has a sensitivity and specificity of 89% for distinguishing bacterial from aseptic meningitis in children?
items.explanation: Both CSF protein >0.5 g/L and serum procalcitonin levels >0.5 ng/mL have these sensitivity and specificity values.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which virus is most commonly associated with large epidemics of neurologic disease outside the United States, particularly in Southeast Asia?
items.explanation: EV71 has produced large epidemics of neurologic disease outside the United States, especially in Southeast Asia.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical CSF profile for enteroviral meningitis?
options: Lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose and elevated protein
Normal leukocyte count with high protein
items.explanation: The CSF profile is typically a lymphocytic pleocytosis (100–1000 cells/μL) with normal glucose and normal or mildly elevated protein concentration.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.question: In which season are arbovirus infections most likely to occur?
items.explanation: Arbovirus infections occur predominantly in the summer and early fall.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.question: Which diagnostic procedure is considered the most sensitive for detecting enteroviral meningitis?
items.explanation: CSF reverse transcriptase PCR (RT-PCR) is the diagnostic procedure of choice for enteroviral meningitis, with sensitivity >95%.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.question: Which virus is responsible for the majority of cases of HSV encephalitis?
items.explanation: HSV-1 is responsible for 90% of cases of HSV encephalitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary diagnostic method for HSV meningitis?
items.explanation: Diagnosis of HSV meningitis is usually by HSV CSF PCR.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.question: Which cranial nerves are most commonly involved in HIV meningitis?
items.explanation: Cranial nerve palsies involving cranial nerves V, VII, or VIII are more common in HIV meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.question: What is the typical time frame for onset of vaccine-associated mumps meningitis after vaccination?
items.explanation: Vaccine-associated mumps meningitis typically occurs 2–4 weeks after vaccination.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis
items.question: Which clinical findings are associated with LCMV infection in patients presenting with aseptic meningitis?
options: Leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and abnormal liver function tests
Hypoglycorrhachia (<30%) with a marked CSF pleocytosis (>1000 cells/μL)
items.explanation: LCMV infection can present with leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, abnormal liver function tests, hypoglycorrhachia (<30%), and marked CSF pleocytosis (>1000 cells/μL).
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis
items.question: What is the recommended treatment for patients with suspected bacterial meningitis while awaiting culture results?
options: Symptomatic treatment only, including analgesics and antipyretics
items.explanation: Patients with suspected bacterial meningitis should receive appropriate empirical therapy pending culture results.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis
items.question: Which patients are advised to be hospitalized for viral meningitis?
options: Immunocompetent patients with no focal signs or symptoms and a classic CSF profile
Patients with significant alteration in consciousness, seizures, or focal signs suggesting encephalitis
All immunocompromised patients regardless of symptoms
Patients with typical viral meningitis who have adequate home monitoring
items.explanation: Immunocompromised patients and those with significant alteration in consciousness, seizures, or focal signs suggesting encephalitis should be hospitalized.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis

items.question: Which of the following is a common causative organism of subacute meningitis?
items.explanation: M. tuberculosis is listed as one of the common causative organisms for subacute meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is a typical clinical manifestation in patients with subacute meningitis?
items.explanation: Patients with subacute meningitis typically have an unrelenting headache.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is the prognosis for full recovery from viral meningitis in adults?
items.explanation: The prognosis for full recovery from viral meningitis in adults is excellent.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic CSF abnormality in tuberculous meningitis?
items.explanation: Tuberculous meningitis typically presents with lymphocytic pleocytosis, not neutrophilic.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the gold standard for diagnosing tuberculous meningitis?
items.explanation: Cultures of CSF are considered the gold standard for diagnosing tuberculous meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In which condition is a reactive CSF cryptococcal antigen test highly sensitive and specific?
items.explanation: A reactive CSF cryptococcal antigen test is highly sensitive and specific for cryptococcal meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the initial empirical therapy for tuberculous meningitis?
options: Isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol, and pyridoxine
Aqueous penicillin G intravenously every 4 hours
items.explanation: Initial therapy for tuberculous meningitis includes isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol, and pyridoxine.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.question: When can pyrazinamide be discontinued in the treatment of tuberculous meningitis?
options: After 2 weeks if clinical response is good
After 8 weeks if clinical response is good
After 6 months regardless of clinical response
items.explanation: Pyrazinamide can be discontinued after 8 weeks if the clinical response to treatment is good.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.question: What therapy is recommended for HIV-negative patients with tuberculous meningitis?
items.explanation: Dexamethasone therapy is recommended for HIV-negative patients with tuberculous meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.question: What is the treatment duration of fluconazole following induction therapy in non-HIV, nontransplant patients with C. neoformans meningitis?
items.explanation: Following induction therapy, fluconazole is used for consolidation therapy over 8 weeks in non-HIV, nontransplant patients with C. neoformans meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.statement: Neisseria meningitidis is responsible for recurring epidemics of meningitis every 8–12 years.
items.explanation: N. meningitidis causes recurring epidemics of meningitis approximately every 8–12 years.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The annual incidence of bacterial meningitis in the United States is ~5 cases/100,000 population.
items.explanation: The correct annual incidence is approximately 1.4 cases per 100,000 population.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The serogroup B polysaccharide capsule is highly immunogenic.
items.explanation: The serogroup B polysaccharide capsule is poorly immunogenic.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Meningococcal vaccines reduce nasopharyngeal carriage of meningococci for all serogroups.
items.explanation: The vaccines do not significantly reduce nasopharyngeal carriage for group B, but they do for groups A, C, W, and Y.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Neutrophil degranulation contributes to cytotoxic edema during bacterial meningitis.
items.explanation: Neutrophil degranulation releases toxic metabolites that contribute to cytotoxic edema.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: CSF leukocytes are the primary contributors to bacterial clearance in meningitis.
items.explanation: Contrary to previous beliefs, CSF leukocytes probably do little to contribute to the clearance of CSF bacterial infection.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs have high sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing meningeal irritation.
items.explanation: The sensitivity and specificity of Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs are uncertain.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: Petechiae in meningococcemia can be found on the palms and soles.
items.explanation: Petechiae of meningococcemia may occasionally appear on the palms and soles.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: CSF glucose concentrations <2.2 mmol/L (<40 mg/dL) are considered normal.
items.explanation: CSF glucose concentrations <2.2 mmol/L (<40 mg/dL) are abnormal.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Administration of 50 mL of 50% glucose (D50) prior to LP significantly alters CSF glucose concentration.
items.explanation: The administration of D50 is unlikely to alter CSF glucose concentration significantly unless more than a few hours have elapsed.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Patients with ehrlichial infections typically present with leukocytosis.
items.explanation: Ehrlichial infections are associated with laboratory evidence of leukopenia, not leukocytosis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Subarachnoid hemorrhage is a noninfectious CNS disorder that can mimic bacterial meningitis.
items.explanation: Subarachnoid hemorrhage is considered in the differential diagnosis of bacterial meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Ceftriaxone provides adequate coverage for P. aeruginosa in neurosurgical patients.
items.explanation: Ceftriaxone does not provide adequate activity against CNS infection with P. aeruginosa; ceftazidime or meropenem should be used instead.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · EMPIRICAL ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.statement: Meropenem is highly active in vitro against L. monocytogenes.
items.explanation: Meropenem has been demonstrated to be effective in cases of meningitis caused by P. aeruginosa and shows good activity against penicillin-resistant pneumococci.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · EMPIRICAL ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.statement: Rifampin is recommended for chemoprophylaxis in pregnant women exposed to meningococcal meningitis.
items.explanation: Rifampin is not recommended in pregnant women; alternatives include azithromycin or ceftriaxone.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.statement: A 2-week course of intravenous antimicrobial therapy is recommended for meningococcal meningitis.
items.explanation: A 7-day course is adequate for uncomplicated meningococcal meningitis, not 2 weeks.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.statement: Dexamethasone reduces the number of unfavorable outcomes in patients with pneumococcal meningitis.
items.explanation: Clinical trials have shown that dexamethasone reduced unfavorable outcomes, including death, particularly in patients with pneumococcal meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY

items.statement: Dexamethasone is equally effective in preventing neurologic sequelae in both high- and low-income countries.
items.explanation: The efficacy of dexamethasone therapy differs between high- and low-income countries, with trials in low-income countries showing no benefit.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Severe sequelae occur in approximately 25% of survivors of bacterial meningitis.
items.explanation: Moderate or severe sequelae occur in ~25% of survivors, not severe.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Profound alterations in consciousness are typical of viral meningitis.
items.explanation: Profound alterations in consciousness suggest encephalitis or another diagnosis, not viral meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Viral meningitis is a nationally reportable disease.
items.explanation: Viral meningitis is not a nationally reportable disease.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The incidence of viral meningitis in the United States is estimated to be at least 75,000 cases per year.
items.explanation: It has been estimated that the incidence is at least 75,000 cases per year.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: CSF glucose concentration is typically decreased in viral meningitis.
items.explanation: The CSF glucose concentration is typically normal in viral infections, though it may be decreased in some cases like mumps or LCMV.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: CSF cultures are highly sensitive for diagnosing viral meningitis.
items.explanation: The sensitivity of CSF cultures for the diagnosis of viral meningitis is generally poor.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Neuroimaging studies are mandatory for all patients with suspected viral meningitis.
items.explanation: Neuroimaging is not necessary in uncomplicated cases but should be performed under certain conditions.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The bacterial meningitis score can help assess the probability of bacterial meningitis.
items.explanation: This scoring system helps determine the likelihood of bacterial meningitis in children with certain CSF findings.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Enteroviral meningitis is most common in adults.
items.explanation: Enteroviral meningitis is most likely to occur in children (<15 years).
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.statement: CSF RT-PCR for WNV has higher sensitivity than CSF serology.
items.explanation: The sensitivity of CSF RT-PCR (~70%) is less than that of CSF serology for WNV.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.statement: EBV is almost never cultured from CSF.
items.explanation: EBV is almost never cultured from CSF.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.statement: The presence of parotitis excludes the diagnosis of mumps meningitis.
items.explanation: Parotitis is suggestive but not required for diagnosing mumps meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis
items.statement: Intravenous acyclovir is recommended for all patients with viral meningitis.
items.explanation: Intravenous acyclovir may be beneficial in cases of HSV-1 or -2, severe EBV, or VZV infection, but not universally for all viral meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis
items.statement: Vaccination can prevent neurologic complications associated with mumps and measles.
items.explanation: Vaccination is effective in preventing meningitis and other neurologic complications from poliovirus, mumps, measles, rubella, and varicella infections.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 3) | Acute Viral Meningitis

items.statement: Tuberculous meningitis in adults develops acutely from hematogenous spread of tubercle bacilli to the meninges.
items.explanation: Tuberculous meningitis does not develop acutely; it involves miliary tubercles forming in brain parenchyma.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: C. neoformans is the most common pathogen causing fungal meningitis.
items.explanation: The passage states that C. neoformans is the most common pathogen causing fungal meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: The sensitivity of nucleic acid amplification tests like XpertMTB/RIF Ultra for tuberculous meningitis has been well defined.
items.explanation: The consensus is that the sensitivity and risk of false-negative results have not been defined.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: A negative CSF VDRL test rules out neurosyphilis.
items.explanation: A negative CSF VDRL does not rule out neurosyphilis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Patients with HIV infection are treated with AmB or a lipid formulation plus flucytosine for at least 2 weeks, followed by fluconazole for a minimum of 8 weeks.
items.explanation: HIV-infected patients receive AmB or a lipid formulation plus flucytosine initially, then fluconazole for at least 8 weeks.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.statement: The standard criterion for treatment success in syphilitic meningitis is the normalization of CSF cell count within 6 months.
items.explanation: CSF should be reexamined at 6-month intervals for 2 years, with cell count expected to normalize within 12 months.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.assertion: Individuals with complement deficiencies are at increased risk for meningococcal disease.
items.reason: Complement components are necessary to lyse meningococci by both classic and alternative pathways.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why individuals with complement deficiencies are at increased risk.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Group B streptococcus is predominantly responsible for meningitis in neonates.
items.reason: It has been reported with increasing frequency in individuals aged >50 years, particularly those with underlying diseases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why Group B streptococcus is predominantly responsible for meningitis in neonates.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: The neurologic injury in bacterial meningitis can progress even after CSF sterilization.
items.reason: Neurologic manifestations result from the immune response rather than direct bacteria-induced tissue injury.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.assertion: Inflammatory cytokines decrease the permeability of cerebral capillary endothelial cells.
items.reason: Cytokines upregulate selectins on endothelial cells, promoting leukocyte adherence and increasing vascular permeability.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; inflammatory cytokines increase, not decrease, the permeability of cerebral capillary endothelial cells.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.assertion: A CSF/serum glucose ratio <0.4 is highly suggestive of bacterial meningitis.
items.reason: This ratio corrects for hyperglycemia that may mask a relative decrease in the CSF glucose concentration.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why a low ratio is suggestive of bacterial meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: PCR assays can replace CSF bacteria culture in diagnosing bacterial meningitis.
items.reason: PCR assays have rapid turnaround times and detect nucleic acids of common pathogens.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because PCR assays cannot replace cultures, which are needed for antimicrobial susceptibility testing.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: MRI should be performed promptly in patients with suspected meningitis who have focal features.
items.reason: To detect intracranial infection and search for associated areas of infection in the sinuses or mastoid bones.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why MRI is important in cases with focal neurologic findings.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: HSV encephalitis can be distinguished from bacterial meningitis by its CSF profile.
items.reason: Bacterial meningitis typically presents with lymphocytic pleocytosis and normal glucose levels in the CSF.
items.explanation: The reason is false; bacterial meningitis presents with PMN pleocytosis and hypoglycorrhachia.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: For S. pneumoniae meningitis with cefotaxime or ceftriaxone MICs ≤0.5 μg/mL, treatment with these antibiotics is usually adequate.
items.reason: Isolates of S. pneumoniae that have cephalosporins MICs ≤0.5 μg/mL are considered sensitive to the cephalosporins.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.assertion: Intrathecal administration of vancomycin is preferred over intraventricular for adequate concentrations in cerebral ventricles.
items.reason: Adequate concentrations of vancomycin in the cerebral ventricles are not always achieved with intrathecal administration.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; intraventricular administration is preferred over intrathecal for adequate concentrations.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.subtopic: INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.assertion: Decreased CSF glucose concentration is predictive of increased mortality in bacterial meningitis.
items.reason: Markedly increased CSF protein concentration has been associated with poorer outcomes in some series.
items.explanation: Both decreased CSF glucose and increased CSF protein are predictors, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · INCREASED INTRACRANIAL PRESSURE | PROGNOSIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: PCR amplification of viral nucleic acid from CSF is more sensitive than viral cultures for diagnosing CNS infections.
items.reason: PCR has become the diagnostic procedure of choice, especially for enteroviral and HSV infections of the CNS.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why PCR is preferred over viral cultures.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Oligoclonal gamma globulin bands in CSF are specific to viral infections.
items.reason: These bands occur with a number of viral infections and are directed against viral proteins.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because oligoclonal bands also occur in nonviral infections and certain noninfectious neurologic diseases.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Meningitis secondary to noninfectious inflammatory diseases can mimic viral meningitis.
items.reason: These conditions include medication-induced hypersensitivity, SLE, and sarcoidosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these conditions can mimic viral meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Enteroviral meningitis can present with a normal CSF leukocyte count.
items.reason: Up to 15% of patients, most commonly young infants, have a normal CSF leukocyte count.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.assertion: WNV meningitis can be distinguished from bacterial meningitis by hypoglycorrhachia.
items.reason: The rarity of hypoglycorrhachia in WNV infection helps distinguish it from bacterial meningitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the absence, not presence, of hypoglycorrhachia distinguishes WNV from bacterial meningitis.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 1)

items.assertion: HSV meningitis occurs in a higher percentage of women than men during the initial episode of genital herpes.
items.reason: HSV meningitis occurs in ~25–35% of women and ~10–15% of men at the time of an initial (primary) episode of genital herpes.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.assertion: Mumps meningitis can be diagnosed by detecting IgG antibodies in CSF.
items.reason: Diagnosis is typically made by culture of virus from CSF or by detecting IgM antibodies or seroconversion.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true; diagnosis involves IgM antibodies, not IgG.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · SPECIFIC VIRAL ETIOLOGIES (part 2)

items.assertion: A 6-month course of therapy is generally acceptable for tuberculous meningitis.
items.reason: Therapy should be prolonged to 9–12 months in patients with inadequate resolution or positive mycobacterial cultures during treatment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why a 6-month course is generally acceptable.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis

items.assertion: Fluconazole monotherapy can be used for C. immitis meningitis.
items.reason: High-dose fluconazole (1000 mg daily) or intravenous AmB is recommended for treatment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because high-dose fluconazole is not monotherapy; it can be used as an alternative to AmB.
items.reference: Karen L Roos Kenneth L Tyler · Subacute Meningitis
